title: Poppy

infobox.value: is a [flowering plant](/wiki/Flowering_plant "Flowering plant") in the subfamily [Papaveroideae](/wiki/Papaveroideae "Papaveroideae") of the family [Papaveraceae](/wiki/Papaveraceae "Papaveraceae"). Poppies are [herbaceous plants](/wiki/Herbaceous_plant "Herbaceous plant"), often grown for their colourful flowers. One species of poppy, *[Papaver somniferum](/wiki/Papaver_somniferum "Papaver somniferum")*, is the source of the narcotic drug mixture [opium](/wiki/Opium "Opium"), which contains powerful medicinal [alkaloids](/wiki/Alkaloid "Alkaloid") such as [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine") and has been used since ancient times as an [analgesic](/wiki/Analgesic "Analgesic") and [narcotic](/wiki/Narcotic "Narcotic") [medicinal](/wiki/Medicine "Medicine") and [recreational drug](/wiki/Recreational_drug "Recreational drug"). It also produces [edible seeds](/wiki/Poppy_seed "Poppy seed"). Following the [trench warfare](/wiki/Trench_warfare "Trench warfare") in the poppy fields of [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders"), Belgium, during [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), poppies, specifically [the red poppy](/wiki/Papaver_rhoeas "Papaver rhoeas"), have become a symbol of [remembrance](/wiki/Remembrance_Day "Remembrance Day") of soldiers who have died during wartime, especially in the UK, Canada, Australia, Africa and other [Commonwealth realms](/wiki/Commonwealth_realms "Commonwealth realms").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Species of flowering plant in the family Papaveraceae

This article is about the plant. For the singer, see [Poppy (singer)](/wiki/Poppy_(singer) "Poppy (singer)"). For other uses, see [Poppy (disambiguation)](/wiki/Poppy_(disambiguation) "Poppy (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Poppies_in_the_Sunset_on_Lake_Geneva.jpg/330px-Poppies_in_the_Sunset_on_Lake_Geneva.jpg)](/wiki/File:Poppies_in_the_Sunset_on_Lake_Geneva.jpg)

Poppies on [Lake Geneva](/wiki/Lake_Geneva "Lake Geneva"), [Montreux](/wiki/Montreux "Montreux")

A **poppy** is a [flowering plant](/wiki/Flowering_plant "Flowering plant") in the subfamily [Papaveroideae](/wiki/Papaveroideae "Papaveroideae") of the family [Papaveraceae](/wiki/Papaveraceae "Papaveraceae"). Poppies are [herbaceous plants](/wiki/Herbaceous_plant "Herbaceous plant"), often grown for their colourful flowers. One species of poppy, *[Papaver somniferum](/wiki/Papaver_somniferum "Papaver somniferum")*, is the source of the narcotic drug mixture [opium](/wiki/Opium "Opium"), which contains powerful medicinal [alkaloids](/wiki/Alkaloid "Alkaloid") such as [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine") and has been used since ancient times as an [analgesic](/wiki/Analgesic "Analgesic") and [narcotic](/wiki/Narcotic "Narcotic") [medicinal](/wiki/Medicine "Medicine") and [recreational drug](/wiki/Recreational_drug "Recreational drug"). It also produces [edible seeds](/wiki/Poppy_seed "Poppy seed"). Following the [trench warfare](/wiki/Trench_warfare "Trench warfare") in the poppy fields of [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders"), Belgium, during [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), poppies, specifically [the red poppy](/wiki/Papaver_rhoeas "Papaver rhoeas"), have become a symbol of [remembrance](/wiki/Remembrance_Day "Remembrance Day") of soldiers who have died during wartime, especially in the UK, Canada, Australia, Africa and other [Commonwealth realms](/wiki/Commonwealth_realms "Commonwealth realms").

## Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poppy&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Description")]

[![A close-up of a bright red poppy flower](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/A_bright_red_poppy_flower.jpg/250px-A_bright_red_poppy_flower.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_bright_red_poppy_flower.jpg)

A close-up of a red-flowered poppy

[![Wild poppy in Behbahan](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Persian_Poppy%2C_Behbahan.jpg/250px-Persian_Poppy%2C_Behbahan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Persian_Poppy,_Behbahan.jpg)

Wild poppy in [Behbahan](/wiki/Behbahan_County "Behbahan County")

[![Wild poppy in Mazandaran, Iran](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Poppies_at_Harrietsham%2C_Kent.jpg/250px-Poppies_at_Harrietsham%2C_Kent.jpg)](/wiki/File:Poppies_at_Harrietsham,_Kent.jpg)

Wild poppy in [Harrietsham](/wiki/Harrietsham "Harrietsham"), [Kent](/wiki/Kent "Kent"), England

Poppies are [herbaceous](/wiki/Herbaceous_plant "Herbaceous plant") [annual](/wiki/Annual_plant "Annual plant"), [biennial](/wiki/Biennial_plant "Biennial plant") or short-lived [perennial](/wiki/Perennial_plant "Perennial plant") plants. Some species are [monocarpic](/wiki/Monocarpic "Monocarpic"), dying after flowering.

Poppies can be over 1 metre (3.3 ft) tall with flowers up to 15 centimetres (5.9 in) across. Flowers of species (not cultivars) have 4 or 6 petals, many [stamens](/wiki/Stamen "Stamen") forming a conspicuous whorl in the center of the flower and an ovary composed of 2 or more fused carpels. The petals are showy, may be of almost any colour and may have markings. The petals are crumpled in the [bud](/wiki/Bud "Bud") and as blooming finishes, the petals often lie flat before falling away.

In the temperate zones, poppies bloom from spring into early summer.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Most species secrete [latex](/wiki/Latex "Latex") when injured. [Bees](/wiki/Bee "Bee") use poppies as a [pollen source](/wiki/Pollen_source "Pollen source"). The [pollen](/wiki/Pollen "Pollen") of the oriental poppy, *[Papaver orientale](/wiki/Papaver_orientale "Papaver orientale")*, is dark blue, that of the field or corn poppy (*[Papaver rhoeas](/wiki/Papaver_rhoeas "Papaver rhoeas")*) is grey to dark green.[[2]](#cite_note-Hodges-2) The opium poppy, *[Papaver somniferum](/wiki/Papaver_somniferum "Papaver somniferum")*, grows wild in [Southeast Europe](/wiki/Southeast_Europe "Southeast Europe") and [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia"). It is believed that it originated in the [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean "Mediterranean") region.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Glaucium_flavum_2015-06-16_442.JPG/250px-Glaucium_flavum_2015-06-16_442.JPG)](/wiki/File:Glaucium_flavum_2015-06-16_442.JPG)

A flowering *[glaucium flavum](/wiki/Glaucium_flavum "Glaucium flavum")*

Poppies belong to the subfamily [Papaveroideae](/wiki/Papaveroideae "Papaveroideae") of the family [Papaveraceae](/wiki/Papaveraceae "Papaveraceae"), which includes the following genera:

* *[Papaver](/wiki/Papaver "Papaver")* – *[Papaver rhoeas](/wiki/Papaver_rhoeas "Papaver rhoeas")*, *[Papaver somniferum](/wiki/Papaver_somniferum "Papaver somniferum")*, *[Papaver orientale](/wiki/Papaver_orientale "Papaver orientale")*, *[Papaver nudicaule](/wiki/Papaver_nudicaule "Papaver nudicaule")*, *[Papaver cambricum](/wiki/Papaver_cambricum "Papaver cambricum")*
* *[Eschscholzia](/wiki/Eschscholzia "Eschscholzia")* – *[Eschscholzia californica](/wiki/Eschscholzia_californica "Eschscholzia californica")*
* *[Meconopsis](/wiki/Meconopsis "Meconopsis")* – *[Meconopsis napaulensis](/wiki/Meconopsis_napaulensis "Meconopsis napaulensis")*
* *[Glaucium](/wiki/Glaucium "Glaucium")* - the horned poppies including *[Glaucium flavum](/wiki/Glaucium_flavum "Glaucium flavum")* and *[Glaucium corniculatum](/wiki/Glaucium_corniculatum "Glaucium corniculatum")*
* *[Stylophorum](/wiki/Stylophorum "Stylophorum")* – celandine poppy
* *[Argemone](/wiki/Argemone "Argemone")* – prickly poppy
* *[Romneya](/wiki/Romneya "Romneya")* – matilija poppy and relatives
* *[Canbya](/wiki/Canbya "Canbya")* – pygmy poppy
* *[Stylomecon](/wiki/Stylomecon "Stylomecon")* – wind poppy
* *[Arctomecon](/wiki/Arctomecon "Arctomecon")* – desert bearpaw poppy
* *[Hunnemannia](/wiki/Hunnemannia "Hunnemannia")* – tulip poppy
* *[Dendromecon](/wiki/Dendromecon "Dendromecon")* – tree poppy

## Uses and cultivation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poppy&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Uses and cultivation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/3mohn_z01.JPG/250px-3mohn_z01.JPG)](/wiki/File:3mohn_z01.JPG)

Poppy stems next to jars of blue, gray, and white poppy seeds used for pastries

The flowers of most poppy species are attractive and are widely cultivated as annual or perennial [ornamental plants](/wiki/Ornamental_plant "Ornamental plant"). This has resulted in a number of commercially important cultivars, such as the Shirley poppy, a cultivar of *Papaver rhoeas* and semi-double or double (flore plena) forms of the opium poppy *Papaver somniferum* and oriental poppy (*Papaver orientale*). Poppies of several other genera are also cultivated in gardens.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Poppy seeds](/wiki/Poppy_seeds "Poppy seeds") are rich in oil, [carbohydrates](/wiki/Carbohydrate "Carbohydrate"), [calcium](/wiki/Calcium "Calcium") and [protein](/wiki/Protein "Protein") and are regularly used to fill various pastries in Central and Eastern Europe. [Poppy oil](/wiki/Poppyseed_oil "Poppyseed oil") is often used as cooking oil, salad dressing oil, or in products such as margarine. Poppy oil can also be added to spices for cakes or breads. Poppy products are also used in different paints, varnishes, and some cosmetics.[[4]](#cite_note-Kryzmanski,_J_1999_p._388-393-4)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Afghan_poppies.jpg/250px-Afghan_poppies.jpg)](/wiki/File:Afghan_poppies.jpg)

Poppy cultivators being interviewed in a poppy field

A few species have other uses, principally as sources of drugs and foods. The [opium poppy](/wiki/Opium_poppy "Opium poppy") is widely cultivated and its worldwide production is monitored by international agencies. It is used for production of dried latex and [opium](/wiki/Opium "Opium"), the principal precursor of narcotic and analgesic [opiates](/wiki/Opiates "Opiates") such as [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine"), [heroin](/wiki/Heroin "Heroin"), and [codeine](/wiki/Codeine "Codeine").

### Traditional medicine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poppy&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Traditional medicine")]

Raw opium poppy seeds can sometimes contain extremely small quantities of opiate alkaloids such as [morphine](/wiki/Morphine "Morphine") and [codeine](/wiki/Codeine "Codeine"). Poppy seeds and fixed oils can also be nonnarcotic because when they are harvested about twenty days after the flower has opened, the morphine is no longer present.[[4]](#cite_note-Kryzmanski,_J_1999_p._388-393-4) Poppy cultivation is strictly regulated in most parts of the world, with exceptions, such as India where opium gum, which also contains the analgesic [thebaine](/wiki/Thebaine "Thebaine"), is legally produced.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poppy&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")]

*Papaver somniferum* was domesticated by [the indigenous people](/wiki/Old_Europe_(archaeology) "Old Europe (archaeology)") of Western and Central Europe between 6000 and 3500 BC.[[6]](#cite_note-6) However, it is believed that its origins may come from the [Sumerian people](/wiki/Sumer "Sumer"), where the first use of opium was recognized.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Poppies and opium made their way around the world along the [silk road](/wiki/Silk_Road "Silk Road").[[8]](#cite_note-8) Juglets resembling poppy seed pods have been discovered with trace amounts of opium and the flower appeared in jewelry and on art pieces in Ancient Egypt, dated 1550–1292 BC.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The eradication of poppy cultivation came about in the early 1900s through international conferences due to safety concerns associated with the production of opium. In the 1970s the American [war on drugs](/wiki/War_on_drugs "War on drugs") targeted [Turkish](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey") production of the plant, leading to a more negative popular opinion of the U.S.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## In culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poppy&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: In culture")]

The girl's given name "Poppy" is taken from the name of the flower.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

A poppy flower is depicted on the [reverse](/wiki/Obverse_and_reverse "Obverse and reverse") of the Macedonian 500-[denar](/wiki/Macedonian_denar "Macedonian denar") banknote, issued in 1996 and 2003.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The poppy is also part of the [coat of arms of North Macedonia](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_North_Macedonia "Coat of arms of North Macedonia").

Canada has issued special quarters (25-cent coins) with a red poppy on the reverse in 2004, 2008, 2010, and 2015. The 2004 Canadian "poppy" quarter was the world's first coloured circulation coin.[[14]](#cite_note-mint-14)

## Symbolism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poppy&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Symbolism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Vincent_van_Gogh_-_Field_with_Poppies_%281889%29.jpg/250px-Vincent_van_Gogh_-_Field_with_Poppies_%281889%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vincent_van_Gogh_-_Field_with_Poppies_(1889).jpg)

*Field with Poppies*, 1889, by [Vincent van Gogh](/wiki/Vincent_van_Gogh "Vincent van Gogh")

Poppies have long been used as a symbol of sleep, peace, and [death](/wiki/Death "Death"): Sleep because the opium extracted from them is a [sedative](/wiki/Sedative "Sedative"), and death because of the common blood-red colour of the red poppy in particular.[[15]](#cite_note-15) In [Greek](/wiki/Greek_mythology "Greek mythology") and [Roman](/wiki/Roman_mythology "Roman mythology") myths, poppies were used as offerings to the dead.[[16]](#cite_note-Baum-16) Poppies used as emblems on [tombstones](/wiki/Headstone "Headstone") symbolize eternal sleep. This symbolism was evoked in [L. Frank Baum](/wiki/L._Frank_Baum "L. Frank Baum")'s 1900 children's novel *[The Wonderful Wizard of Oz](/wiki/The_Wonderful_Wizard_of_Oz "The Wonderful Wizard of Oz")*, in which a magical poppy field threatened to make the protagonists sleep forever.[[16]](#cite_note-Baum-16)
A second interpretation of poppies in [Classical mythology](/wiki/Classical_mythology "Classical mythology") is that the bright scarlet colour signifies a promise of [resurrection](/wiki/Resurrection "Resurrection") after death.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Red-flowered poppy is unofficially considered the national flower of the [Albanians](/wiki/Albanians "Albanians") in [Albania](/wiki/Albania "Albania"), [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo "Kosovo") and elsewhere. This is due to its red and black colours, the same as the colours of the flag of Albania. Red poppies are also the national flower of [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"). The California poppy, *[Eschscholzia californica](/wiki/Eschscholzia_californica "Eschscholzia californica")*, is the state flower of California.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

The powerful symbolism of *Papaver rhoeas* has been borrowed by various advocacy campaigns, such as the [White Poppy](/wiki/White_Poppy "White Poppy") and [Simon Topping](/wiki/Simon_Topping "Simon Topping")'s black poppy.

### Wartime remembrance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Poppy&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Wartime remembrance")]

Main article: [Remembrance poppy](/wiki/Remembrance_poppy "Remembrance poppy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Lest_we_forget.jpg/250px-Lest_we_forget.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lest_we_forget.jpg)

A Canadian [remembrance poppy](/wiki/Remembrance_poppy "Remembrance poppy") worn on the lapel

The [poppy of wartime remembrance](/wiki/Remembrance_poppy "Remembrance poppy") is *[Papaver rhoeas](/wiki/Papaver_rhoeas "Papaver rhoeas")*, the red-flowered corn poppy. This poppy is a common [plant](/wiki/Ruderal_species "Ruderal species") of [disturbed ground](/wiki/Disturbed_ground "Disturbed ground") in Europe and is found in many locations, including [Flanders](/wiki/Flanders "Flanders"), which is the setting of the famous poem "[In Flanders Fields](/wiki/In_Flanders_Fields "In Flanders Fields")" by the Canadian surgeon and soldier [John McCrae](/wiki/John_McCrae "John McCrae"). In Canada, the United Kingdom, Australia, South Africa and New Zealand, artificial poppies (plastic in Canada, paper in the UK, Australia, South Africa, Malta and New Zealand) are worn to commemorate those who died in war.

This form of commemoration is associated with [Remembrance Day](/wiki/Remembrance_Day "Remembrance Day"), which falls on 11 November. In Canada, Australia and the UK, poppies are often worn from the beginning of November through to the 11th, or Remembrance Sunday, if that falls on a later date. In New Zealand and Australia, soldiers are also commemorated on [ANZAC day](/wiki/ANZAC_day "ANZAC day") (25 April),[[19]](#cite_note-Seal2004-19) although the poppy is still commonly worn around Remembrance Day. Wearing of poppies has been a custom since 1924 in the United States.[[20]](#cite_note-20) [Moina Michael](/wiki/Moina_Michael "Moina Michael") of Georgia is credited as the founder of the Memorial Poppy in the United States.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-BookInc2003-22)[[23]](#cite_note-United_States_of_America_Congressional_Record-23)

The [Veterans of Foreign Wars](/wiki/Veterans_of_Foreign_Wars "Veterans of Foreign Wars") provides financial aid to its members in need and their families, partly through the selling of artificial poppies known by the registered trademark "Buddy Poppy" since 1924.[[24]](#cite_note-vfw-24) Disabled veterans have assembled the poppies from the beginning of the campaign in 1923.[[24]](#cite_note-vfw-24) The VFW Buddy Poppy program has raised enormous capital to support the welfare of veterans and their families.[[24]](#cite_note-vfw-24)

The original poppy poster was painted by [Howard Chandler Christy](/wiki/Howard_Chandler_Christy "Howard Chandler Christy") in 1926. Many other official posters appeared in subsequent years. Their purpose was to advertise and promote sales.[[25]](#cite_note-25)
